items.title: Distance Metrics
items.description: k-NN ranks neighbors by distance d(x, xᵢ). Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, and cosine choices change which points count as nearest. Distance Metrics is the canonical home for comparing metrics in k-NN and SVM contexts; this topic assumes a metric is chosen and focuses on the k-NN prediction rule.

items.title: Norms and Distances
items.description: Formal definitions of L₁, L₂, and general norms underpin distance computations in k-NN. Norms and Distances defines ||x − xᵢ||; here we use those distances only to select the k closest training indices.

items.title: Scaling and Normalization
items.description: Unscaled features with different ranges dominate distance and distort neighbor sets. Standardization or normalization before k-NN is standard practice — see Scaling and Normalization for methods; this topic treats scaled features as input to the k-NN algorithm.

items.title: Model Complexity
items.description: k acts as the main complexity knob: small k yields flexible boundaries (low bias, high variance); large k smooths predictions (higher bias, lower variance). Model Complexity is the canonical home for bias–variance framing; k-NN is a concrete instance.

items.title: Support Vector Machines
items.description: SVM learns a sparse set of support vectors and a margin-maximizing boundary during training; k-NN stores all training points and decides locally at query time. Both can produce nonlinear boundaries but with opposite compute tradeoffs.
